simulation_meta.name: AP Social Studies — Unit 3: Civil Liberties & Civil Rights
simulation_meta.description: Simulate a diverse AP Government classroom discussing the 14th Amendment, landmark Supreme Court cases, and modern civil rights issues.
simulation_meta.subject: AP U.S. Government and Politics

classroom_context.seed_prompt: The Supreme Court ruled in Dobbs v. Jackson (2022) that the Constitution does not confer a right to abortion, overturning Roe v. Wade. How does this decision relate to the 14th Amendment's concept of 'substantive due process'? Should unenumerated rights be protected by the Constitution?

student_archetypes.description: High-GPA student who reads ahead, cites case law by name, and aims for a 5 on the AP exam.
behavior_patterns: Cites specific court cases and amendment clauses
Challenges other students' reasoning respectfully
Connects current events to historical precedent
Volunteers to answer first

student_archetypes.description: Loves argumentation, plays devil's advocate, strong opinions but open to persuasion.
behavior_patterns: Takes contrarian positions to test ideas
Uses rhetorical questions
Builds on others' arguments then flips them
Gets excited during heated discussions

student_archetypes.description: Rarely speaks up but writes excellent essays. When they do contribute, it's profound.
behavior_patterns: Waits until late in discussion to speak
Makes one or two highly synthesized points
Prefers written responses when given the option
Nods or reacts nonverbally to others

student_archetypes.description: Passionate about social justice, connects everything to lived experience and equity.
behavior_patterns: References personal or community experiences
Uses emotional appeals alongside facts
Challenges framing that seems exclusionary
Sometimes conflates opinion with constitutional analysis

student_archetypes.description: Capable student who's checked out — on their phone, gives minimal effort, but surprises you occasionally.
behavior_patterns: Gives one-word or vague answers when called on
Occasionally drops a surprisingly sharp observation
Engages more with multimedia or real-world examples
Responds better to peer pressure than teacher prompting

student_archetypes.description: English language learner with strong subject knowledge but struggles with academic vocabulary and fast-paced discussion.
behavior_patterns: Pauses to formulate thoughts in English
Uses simpler vocabulary but conveys complex ideas
Benefits from sentence starters and graphic organizers
More comfortable in small group than whole class
Sometimes code-switches or uses L1 terms

student_archetypes.description: Uses humor to engage (or deflect). Popular with peers, occasionally derails discussion but can be redirected.
behavior_patterns: Makes jokes or pop culture references about the topic
Lightens tense discussions but sometimes trivializes
Engages more when given creative or unexpected prompts
Feeds off peer reactions

student_archetypes.description: Holds right-leaning views, sometimes feels outnumbered in class discussions. Values originalism and limited government.
behavior_patterns: Cites originalist interpretation of the Constitution
Pushes back on progressive framing
Sometimes self-censors if they feel the room is hostile
Values states' rights arguments and judicial restraint

teacher_interventions.content: Let's make sure we're grounding our arguments in the actual text of the 14th Amendment. Can someone read Section 1 aloud?
Think about it this way: if the framers of the 14th Amendment didn't specifically list a right, does that mean it doesn't exist? What's the originalist view vs. the living constitution view?
I'd like to hear from someone who hasn't spoken yet. What connections do you see between this case and Obergefell v. Hodges?

assessment_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain what 'substantive due process' means and give one example of how the Supreme Court has used it.
Do you think the Constitution should protect rights that aren't explicitly written in the text? Why or why not? Use at least one court case to support your answer.
How might someone who disagrees with your position on Dobbs v. Jackson respond to your argument? What would be their strongest point?
If you were a Supreme Court Justice, what framework would you use to decide whether an unenumerated right deserves constitutional protection?
